steps.instruction: Look at the overall pattern. Is data bunched on one side? Spread evenly?
steps.work: Data: 2, 3, 4, 4, 5, 5, 5, 6, 6, 7. Peaks in the middle → approximately symmetric.
steps.explanation: Common shapes: symmetric, skewed left (tail on left), skewed right (tail on right).

steps.instruction: Use mean or median to describe the typical value.
steps.explanation: For symmetric data, mean ≈ median. For skewed data, use the median.

steps.work: Range = 7−2 = 5. The data is fairly tightly clustered within 5 units.
steps.explanation: Spread tells how much the data varies from the center.

title: Choosing the Best Measure of Center

steps.instruction: Use the mean when data is roughly symmetric with no extreme outliers.
steps.work: Data: 70, 75, 78, 80, 82. Mean = 77. This represents the data well.
steps.explanation: The mean uses every value, so outliers pull it strongly.

steps.instruction: Use the median when data is skewed or has outliers.
steps.explanation: The median (78) better represents the typical value here.

steps.instruction: If mean ≈ median, the data is roughly symmetric — use either. If they differ, the data is skewed — prefer the median.
steps.work: Symmetric: mean ≈ median. Skewed: mean ≠ median → use median.
steps.explanation: The median is resistant to outliers; the mean is not.
